<insights>

- High coupling (0.57) — 10 imports target "analysis-engine"
- louvain.ts, zone-hash.ts, and zones.ts are a tightly scoped triad with a clear single responsibility — this is a good candidate for eventual extraction into its own sub-package if the algorithm needs reuse elsewhere.
- The three zone-related test files cover detection, size policy, and subdivision separately, indicating the subdivision and size-policy logic is complex enough to warrant dedicated tests — verify these edge cases are exercised with realistic graph inputs.
- With 10 imports flowing into the main Analysis Engine zone and 3 into the Branch Work Analytics zone, this zone is a net consumer of shared types; keeping its own exports minimal protects the rest of the codebase from graph-algorithm churn.
- zone-hash.ts appears as an entrypoint, suggesting external code addresses it directly; if zone hashing is purely an implementation detail of the zones.ts service, making it private would reduce the exposed surface area.
- Cohesion of 0.43 is close to the warning threshold; the zone-size-policy logic may be pulling in enough unrelated dependencies to warrant splitting it into a dedicated policy module.
- Louvain community detection is a well-isolated algorithm here with no outward coupling to the CLI or schema layers, which is architecturally clean.
- Zone-detection-engine has zero outbound imports to the CLI layer, meaning its graph-algorithm logic is consumed exclusively by the analysis-engine zone — this single-consumer pattern is a clean architectural boundary worth preserving explicitly.
- The zone's 10 outbound imports all target analysis-engine, making it a strict leaf-to-core dependency with no fanout, which is a healthy architectural signal that separates it from the more problematic bidirectional zones.
- Zero imports from this zone reach the CLI or schema layers — it is a pure algorithmic leaf with a single consumer (analysis-engine). This clean single-direction dependency should be preserved and documented as a constraint.
- All 10 outbound imports target the analysis-engine zone with nothing flowing back into the CLI layer, making zone-detection-engine the only non-trivial zone with a strictly unidirectional import profile.
- zone-hash.ts is classified with the 'utility' archetype but existing insights note it 'appears as an entrypoint' used by external zones — the archetype label and the observed access pattern are contradictory. Either the archetype is wrong (it is genuinely public and should be documented as such) or the external imports are wrong (it is private and should be accessed only through zones.ts or src/public.ts). One of the two must change; leaving both in place gives tooling inconsistent signals.
- zone-hash.ts is classified 'utility' but accessed directly as an entrypoint by external zones, creating a contradiction between its archetype and its observed import profile. Either correct the archetype to reflect its public role, or collapse it into zones.ts and remove the external import surface to restore its private status.
- zone-detection-engine and schema-validation share identical cohesion (0.43) and coupling (0.57) metrics but for structurally opposite reasons: zone-detection-engine's low cohesion reflects genuine algorithmic complexity with clean unidirectional flow, while schema-validation's reflects co-membership of incompatible file types. Metric-based triage will treat them as equally unhealthy, but they require opposite interventions — zone-detection-engine needs no structural change, schema-validation needs immediate decomposition.
- The '-engine' suffix is shared only by 'analysis-engine' and 'zone-detection-engine'; the other two zones use '-pipeline' and '-validation' suffixes. No consistent zone-name suffix convention is documented, making zone purpose harder to infer from the name alone across the full zone map.
- zone-detection-engine's cohesion score (0.43) is misleadingly low given its architecturally clean profile (strictly unidirectional imports, single algorithmic responsibility). Document explicitly that this zone's cohesion is suppressed by co-membership with its own unit tests, not by coupling debt — otherwise automated health reports will incorrectly flag it as a refactoring candidate alongside the genuinely fragile schema-validation zone.
- Establish a documented naming convention for zone IDs: both 'analysis-engine' and 'zone-detection-engine' use an '-engine' suffix but other zones do not. Standardizing on a pattern (e.g., noun-phrase describing primary responsibility, no suffix) would make the zone map self-describing without requiring zone-level documentation to interpret names.
- [call graph] 232 internal calls, 583 outgoing, 50 incoming (cohesion: 0.28, coupling: 0.72)

</insights>
